goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

morning_briefing.morning_message:
Hey Ameena — you're feeling fantastic this morning with a 93, and your energy mode is high. That's great to see.

Last night was solid: you got 6.2 hours with excellent efficiency (91.87%), and your sleep onset was way earlier than usual — that's a big win. It looks like managing your caffeine timing yesterday really helped there. Yesterday's activity was huge — over 17,700 steps — but you also had long sedentary stretches (839 minutes). That's a pattern of intense bursts followed by sitting, which can leave you feeling drained later even if you're moving a lot. Your nutrition yesterday was moderate: 1445 calories with 92.5g protein, which is decent, but it's a bit below your usual intake.

The standout thing is your mindset. Even though you noted feeling 'Tense' and 'Annoyed', your focus, energy, social, and coping scores were all **100**. That's incredible — it means you're handling any underlying stress with serious resilience. Today's about protecting that high-functioning state while giving your body the support it needs after a big activity day.

Your actions today focus on keeping your sleep momentum going, adding a protein bump to your dinner, and breaking up those long sedentary blocks.

You've got the mental game locked in — now let's match that with the physical fuel and rhythm.

assumptions: High energy/focus scores reflect capacity for structured action
Recent protein/calorie deficits are not intentional
Sleep onset time improvement is sustainable
Missing goals data will be addressed
User can control caffeine and meal timing

evidence_used.goal_focus: sleep improvement with hard schedule constraints

soft_constraints: consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend
follow up on missing goals data
caution with high energy expenditure given recent calorie/protein deficits
leverage high mood/focus for sleep routine implementation

generated_actions.title: Set a 9:15 PM screen-off reminder to protect your wind-down
generated_actions.rationale: Last night's sleep onset was excellent (66 minutes, a sharp positive trend from baseline). Your caffeine was managed yesterday (no caffeine after 2pm). To lock in this improvement and leverage your high energy/focus for sleep routine implementation (soft constraint), a structured wind-down will help maintain the early sleep onset trend.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you start your screen-free wind-down at 9:15 PM tonight?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports starting a screen-free wind-down routine at or close to 9:15 PM

assumptions: You have an alarm or reminder system
You have non-screen activities available
generated_actions.description: Set an alarm for 9:15 PM tonight. When it goes off, put away your phone/laptop and begin a non-screen wind-down activity (e.g., reading, light tidying, stretching) for at least 15 minutes before you plan to sleep.

generated_actions.title: Add a protein-rich side to your dinner tonight
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's nutrition shows a calorie deficit (1445 kcal vs baseline 1788) and a positive but modest protein trend (+1.37 slope, now at 92.5g). Adding a protein side addresses the 'nutrition_gap' risk flag and the soft constraint 'caution with high energy expenditure given recent calorie/protein deficits', especially after a day with 6am basketball practice.

evaluation.success_definition: Daily protein intake reaches at least 100g

assumptions: You have access to protein-rich foods
generated_actions.description: When you have dinner, add a serving of a high-protein food like Greek yogurt, cottage cheese, or a handful of almonds alongside your main meal.

generated_actions.title: Take a 5-minute movement break after 90 minutes of continuous work
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's sedentary minutes were high (839, above baseline 675) and trending upward (+54.59 slope), despite high step count. This addresses the soft constraint 'consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend' and fits within your 'tight schedule' and 'high workload' constraints.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take at least one 5-minute movement break after 90+ minutes of continuous work today?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports taking at least one movement break after prolonged sitting

assumptions: You can take short breaks during work
generated_actions.description: When you've been sitting/working continuously for about 90 minutes, stand up and walk around your space for 5 minutes. Stretch your arms, neck, and back.

must_avoid: remaining seated for the entire break

generated_actions.title: After your last sip of water tonight, do 3 minutes of 4-7-8 breathing
generated_actions.rationale: Your stress was low yesterday (31) and coping was maximal (100), but emotions included 'Tense' and 'Annoyed'. Habit-stacking breathing to your last water ritual leverages your high coping capacity to further reduce any residual tension before sleep, supporting your sleep goal.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you complete the 3 minutes of 4-7-8 breathing after your last water tonight?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing the breathing exercise after their last water

assumptions: You drink water in the evening
generated_actions.description: Right after you finish your last drink of water for the evening, sit comfortably and practice 4-7-8 breathing (inhale 4s, hold 7s, exhale 8s) for 3 minutes.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Commit to a 10pm caffeine cutoff
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's caffeine after 2pm (75mg) coincided with very late sleep onset (128 minutes). Sleep onset time is trending earlier (-308.95 slope), but this pattern could reverse if caffeine timing continues. Today's priority is 'sleep_consistency_recovery'—early caffeine cutoff protects tonight's sleep.
assumptions: You can control caffeine intake timing
previous_day_generated_actions.description: Given yesterday's caffeine after 2pm contributed to your late sleep onset (128 minutes), set a firm rule today: no caffeine after 10am. This means finishing any coffee/tea before that time.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Add protein to your afternoon snack
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's nutrition showed significant deficits: protein (66g) and calories (1298 kcal) were well below baseline. Protein is trending downward (-7.0 slope). Adding a protein snack addresses the nutrition gap risk flag and supports energy stability despite your high energy mode.

evaluation.success_definition: Daily protein intake reaches at least 85g (above yesterday's 66g)

assumptions: You have access to protein-rich snacks
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you feel hungry between lunch and dinner, choose a protein-rich snack like Greek yogurt, nuts, or a protein shake instead of carb-heavy options.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Schedule a 15-minute walk after your tight work block
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Despite high steps yesterday (12,122), sedentary minutes were elevated (640) and MVPA was very low (11 minutes). Sedentary trend is positive (+6.45 slope). A scheduled post-work walk addresses the soft constraint 'consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend' without overwhelming your tight schedule.

evaluation.success_definition: Sedentary minutes today are less than or equal to 600 (below yesterday's 640)

assumptions: You can take a short break after completing a work task
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you finish a major work task today, take a 15-minute walk outside or around your building. This counters yesterday's sedentary trend (640 minutes) while respecting your high workload constraint.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Set a 9pm wind-down reminder
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Your sleep onset was extremely late yesterday (128 minutes). While sleep efficiency was excellent (96.67%), consistent late bedtimes create sleep debt. A structured wind-down helps regulate sleep timing, which is critical for recovery after only 3.87 hours of sleep last night.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you start your wind-down routine at 9pm tonight?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports starting a non-screen wind-down routine at 9pm

assumptions: You can set a reminder and have non-screen activities available
previous_day_generated_actions.description: At 9pm tonight, put your phone aside and spend 10 minutes doing a non-screen activity (reading, stretching, light chores) before your intended bedtime.

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Hey Ameena — 93/100 this morning! That’s a fantastic start.

Let’s look at last night: you slept **3.87 hours**. That’s way short, but your sleep was incredibly efficient (96.67%) with almost no tossing around. Your body did a great job squeezing recovery out of a short window — your HRV is strong at 100.143 and your resting heart rate is steady. Still, that’s a big deficit compared to your usual ~6 hours, and yesterday’s caffeine after 2pm likely played into the late bedtime. Today’s energy might feel high now, but that short sleep can catch up later.

Yesterday’s nutrition was a notable gap: **1298 calories** and **66g protein**, both well below your baseline. Yet you walked over 12,000 steps and reported maximum energy and mood — that’s a mismatch worth watching. Your stress was super low (15/100) and you felt Content, Happy, Excited, which is awesome, but it’s important to fuel that positive state.

Given all this, today’s focus is on **protecting tonight’s sleep** and **supporting your energy with better fuel**, so you don’t run on fumes later. You’ve got the mood and recovery to back it up.

Lean on that excellent HRV and low stress to make today’s adjustments feel smooth.

evidence_quotes: Today was a nice day. It was productive but not stressful.
I need to figure out how to make them stop hurting

meal_descriptions.description: Mixed green and cooked salmon salad
Grilled chicken breast and fries and 3 sugar free dark chocolate cubes
